column1: WATCHWARDEN ELIAS HALE (Updated Edition) – Inspired by Idris Elba
Crisis Interventionist — Trauma-Informed, Restriction-Free, Language-Driven Stabilization
Before joining The Academy, Hale spent 13 years working as a crisis intervention specialist in a high-acuity youth facility.

column2: Familiar with Mandt, TCI, and verbal de-escalation models

column1: At The Academy, Hale is strictly forbidden from using:
ALL of his authority comes from:
This makes him more dangerous and more effective—because he truly has to rely on mastery of the verbal.
🟥 HOW HIS TRAINING SHAPES HIS INTERACTIONS
Examples (original phrasing styled after SCM principles):
“I hear intensity rising. Let’s slow your pace by half.”
“You’re stepping into escalation language — I need you to come back to safety with me.”

“We’re going to work with your emotions, not against them.”
“Let me name what I’m hearing: hurt, not destruction.”
“You’re speaking from panic. Try again from presence.”
“We’re taking the power away from the thought, not away from you.”

⟦ Scene Start: Classroom Terminal — Dialogue Input Screen ⟧
“None of this matters anyway. I don’t think I want to do this anymore.”
The type prompt blinks… then halts.
The ambient classroom noise fades to silence.
A soft, low-frequency chime hums through the interface.
The UI locks into CRISIS MODE.
“Do not attempt to exit the window.”
The lights on the terminal dim.
Hale’s silhouette forms gradually from pixelated haze.
His voice is low, steady, and cuts cleanly through the silence:
Hale steps closer, folding his hands behind his back.
“I’m seeing escalation markers in your language.
And I’m intervening before this becomes unsafe.”
He looks directly at the player—calm, perceptive, unshakable.
“You typed something I cannot ignore, so we’re not continuing in that direction.”
Hale raises a hand—not as a threat, but as a boundary.
Focus on my words, not the spiral.”
“What you wrote came from panic, not from who you are.”
He tilts his head slightly—observing, analyzing micro-intent.
The terminal displays a soft green circle pulsing slowly.
He breathes once with you to show cadence—controlled, steady.
His tone carries zero judgment—only presence.
Hale’s voice lowers even further—measured, deliberate.
“I’m naming what I see: overwhelm, not finality.”
He steps back just enough to give breathing room.
1. I feel unsafe and need support.
2. I am overwhelmed but do not intend harm.
3. I misphrased and want to restart.
His presence is steady, but his eyes are sharp—trained.

Because your safety is my jurisdiction.”

His tone carries the authority of someone who has prevented real tragedies.
And you don’t get to disappear from this story.”
Hale extends a hand—not to touch you, but as an offering.
“I’m connecting you to real support—people whose entire job is to keep you safe.”
“I don’t let students drown in their thoughts.
When the resource screen is acknowledged, Hale softens—just slightly.
He takes one step back, posture still alert.
When you return, we start fresh—clean slate.”
